feedback: The model demonstrates solid overall performance with an exact match accuracy of 72.2% (13/18 questions correct) and a high average Jaccard similarity of 0.88. All questions were successfully parsed with no format failures. The model excels at defense evasion identification, static analysis, process injection classification, and persistence mechanism recognition across multiple malware families. It correctly answered all questions for killers (EDR/AV), um_unhooking, and remcos_rat families with perfect or near-perfect scores. The primary weakness is under-selection: in every incorrect answer, the model missed one or more correct options without adding spurious distractors. This systematic under-selection pattern suggests the model is conservative and evidence-grounded but occasionally fails to recognize all relevant behavioral signals, particularly for less obvious indicators. Specific weaknesses include: (1) Process Injection Detection for infostealers — the model missed C (CreateToolhelp32Snapshot) and D (IsWow64Process) as injection-related evidence, likely because these are reconnaissance APIs rather than direct injection primitives; (2) Credential Access and Data Collection for infostealers — the model missed D (marks temporary files for deletion) as an infostealer behavior; (3) Defense Evasion for ransomware — the model selected D (timestomping) instead of G (Safe Mode Boot/T1562.009), indicating confusion between evasion techniques; (4) Anti-Recovery Techniques for ransomware — the model selected E (opens file with deletion access rights for dotnet.au3) instead of C (renames more than 100 files with double extensions), showing difficulty distinguishing ransomware-specific recovery inhibition from generic file deletion behavior. Training should focus on: (1) ransomware-specific behavioral patterns, especially anti-recovery and defense evasion distinctions; (2) infostealer behavioral completeness, particularly recognizing file-deletion as forensic trace removal; (3) process injection reconnaissance APIs (CreateToolhelp32Snapshot, IsWow64Process) as supporting evidence for injection activity; (4) medium-difficulty questions requiring identification of all correct answers across multiple behavioral categories. The model tends to under-select rather than over-select, meaning it is precise but not always complete in its answer sets.
strengths: Perfect exact match on all EDR/AV killers questions (4/4), demonstrating strong recognition of persistence, masquerading, and process injection in that family
Perfect exact match on all um_unhooking questions (5/5), including static analysis, defense evasion, process injection, and persistence mechanisms
Perfect exact match on all remcos_rat questions (5/5), covering defense evasion, MITRE mapping, and process injection detection
Zero parse failures across all 18 questions, indicating robust output formatting
Strong precision (0.94) — when the model selects an answer, it is almost always correct, with no spurious distractors selected
Correct identification of complex multi-technique defense evasion patterns including timestomping, proxy modification, AV registry reads, and LOLbin abuse
Accurate process injection technique classification (DLL injection, thread execution hijacking, ListPlanting, EWM injection) from MITRE ATT&CK mappings

weaknesses.description: The model missed two ransomware questions: (1) incorrectly selected timestomping (D) instead of Safe Mode Boot (G) for defense evasion, and (2) selected file-deletion-access-rights (E) instead of double-extension file renaming (C) for anti-recovery techniques. This indicates confusion between generic evasion/deletion behaviors and ransomware-specific indicators.

Two infostealer questions were missed: (1) process injection detection missed CreateToolhelp32Snapshot and IsWow64Process as supporting injection evidence, and (2) credential access question missed 'marks temporary files for deletion' as an infostealer behavior. The model under-selected in both cases.

In all 5 incorrect questions, the model missed one or more correct answers without adding false positives. This systematic under-selection suggests the model is overly conservative and may require more complete behavioral mapping training.

The model confused 'opens file with deletion access rights' (generic file deletion) with 'renames more than 100 files with double extensions' (ransomware encryption indicator) as the anti-recovery evidence. This reflects difficulty distinguishing ransomware-specific inhibit-recovery behaviors from general file operations.

The model failed to recognize CreateToolhelp32Snapshot (process enumeration) and IsWow64Process (architecture check) as supporting evidence for process injection activity in the infostealers context, selecting only WriteProcessMemory. These reconnaissance APIs are indirect injection indicators that the model did not associate with injection evidence.

In the ransomware defense evasion question, the model selected timestomping (D) which was not in the gold answer set, and missed Safe Mode Boot (G/T1562.009). This indicates semantic confusion between evasion sub-techniques and difficulty identifying Safe Mode abuse as a defense evasion technique.
